questions.question: What is the primary difference between a singly linked list and a doubly linked list?
options: A) A doubly linked list has only one pointer
B) A doubly linked list has only one pointer per node
C) A singly linked list has only one pointer per node
D) A doubly linked list has two pointers per node
questions.explanation: A doubly linked list has two pointers, one for the next node and one for the previous node, whereas a singly linked list only has one pointer.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of inserting a new node at the beginning of a singly linked list?
questions.explanation: The time complexity of inserting a new node at the beginning of a singly linked list is O(1), because we only need to update the head pointer.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a sentinel node in a linked list?
options: A) To mark the end of the list
B) To mark the beginning of the list
questions.explanation: A sentinel node is used to mark the beginning or end of a linked list, making it easier to traverse the list and perform operations.

questions.question: What is the operation that allows us to traverse a linked list in both forward and backward directions?
questions.explanation: The operation that allows us to traverse a linked list in both forward and backward directions is called traversal, and it involves using two pointers, one for each direction.

questions.question: What is the term for a linked list that contains references to itself?
questions.explanation: A self-referential list is a type of linked list that contains references to itself, allowing for more complex and dynamic operations.
